title: High Q Technologies Partners with Creative Biostructure to Scale Quantum-Enabled EPR Protein Mapping - Quantum Computing Report
gist: Waterloo-based startup High Q Technologies partnered with global CRO Creative Biostructure to commercialize its FATHOM quantum-enabled pulsed EPR spectrometer, positioning it as a fast pre-screening tool for pharma before expensive Cryo-EM verification, backed by over $10M in Canadian public funding.

themes.theme: Quantum sensing hardware commercialization in structural biology

insights.claim: High Q Technologies' FATHOM is described as the world's first commercially available quantum-enabled pulsed EPR spectrometer, using superconducting quantum-sensing resonators instead of classical electromagnetic assemblies to map dynamic, disordered protein structures invisible to Cryo-EM, NMR, and X-ray crystallography.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: First-mover commercial claim in quantum-enabled EPR is a differentiation point versus incumbent structural biology tools (Cryo-EM, NMR); investors should verify the 'first commercially available' claim against competitors.
insights.quote: the world’s first commercially available quantum-enabled pulsed EPR spectrometer
insights.locator: Resolving Dynamic Conformational Ensembles via Superconducting Sensors

insights.claim: FATHOM automates EPR sample analysis via multi-sample cartridges, cutting experimental acquisition timelines from weeks to hours and removing the need for in-house EPR expertise that was previously required to tune and calibrate instruments.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A weeks-to-hours throughput improvement is a concrete efficiency metric that could justify premium CRO pricing or displacement of manual EPR workflows — worth tracking adoption rate at CRO partner labs.
insights.quote: reducing experimental acquisition timelines from weeks to hours without demanding in-house EPR expertise

insights.claim: High Q Technologies is positioning FATHOM as a low-cost pre-screening tool to validate sample quality and AlphaFold structural predictions before pharma developers commit time on expensive, room-sized Cryo-EM microscopes.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A cheaper upstream filter to Cryo-EM could capture a new budget line in pharma R&D workflows without displacing Cryo-EM itself — an adjacent-market entry strategy rather than head-on competition.
insights.quote: Pharma developers can use the compact quantum spectrometer to rapidly verify and validate sample quality, computational AlphaFold structural predictions, or point-to-point distance constraints before spending expensive, highly competitive time on high-end, room-sized Cryo-EM microscopes.

insights.claim: High Q Technologies has received $6.5 million from Canada's Strategic Innovation Fund in 2018 and a further $3.75 million commercialization award in 2024 from FedDev Ontario's Regional Quantum Initiative, alongside backing from Quantum Valley Investments.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Over $10M in non-dilutive Canadian public capital de-risks the company's early commercialization runway; a useful funding-history data point for comps on Canadian quantum-hardware startups.
insights.quote: the company received $6.5 million in non-dilutive capital through the Strategic Innovation Fund in 2018, followed by a recent $3.75 million commercialization award in 2024

insights.claim: High Q Technologies already has installations at academic structural biology labs in Canada, the U.S., and the UK, and plans to ship its first commercial systems into mainland Europe in the coming months.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Near-term European commercial shipment is a concrete milestone to track for revenue-stage validation of the FATHOM platform.
insights.quote: High Q plans to ship its first commercial systems directly into mainland Europe in the coming months.
